# Market Edge Cases

Market edge cases product leaders face, each with scenario, real-world example, decision framework, and common mistakes.

---

### 6. Market Timing

**Scenario:** Uncertainty about whether the market is ready for the product.

**Real-world example:** Webvan (1999) was right about grocery delivery but 15 years too early. Microsoft Zune (2006) was right about music players but too late. First-movers win only 37% of categories.

**Decision framework:**
- Separate the "what" from the "when" — a good product at the wrong time still fails
- Check enabling conditions: Is the technology ready? Has user behavior shifted? Is infrastructure available?
- Fast-followers show superior profitability in 42% of cases — being second with better execution can win
- If too early: reduce investment, maintain option value, watch for timing signals
- If too late: require 10x improvement or distribution/bundling advantage to enter

**Common mistakes:** Confusing "people will want this" with "people want this now"; waiting for perfect timing (it doesn't exist); ignoring market readiness signals.

---

### 7. Competitor Response

**Scenario:** A competitor copies your key differentiator.

**Real-world example:** Microsoft Teams vs. Slack. Microsoft copied core features and bundled with Office 365. Slack responded by going enterprise, maintaining 143% NRR.

**Decision framework:**
- Focus on differentiation, not parity — you'll never out-feature an incumbent with more resources
- Identify your moat (see [frameworks-competitive-positioning.md](frameworks-competitive-positioning.md)) and deepen it
- If your differentiation was a feature, you didn't have a moat — build one (data, network effects, community)
- Consider: is the competitor's copy good enough? Sometimes poor copies validate your approach

**Common mistakes:** Entering a feature war with a better-resourced competitor; panicking into reactive development; abandoning your differentiation to match their additions.

---

### 8. International / Multi-Market Expansion

**Scenario:** Taking a product that works domestically to international markets.

**Real-world example:** Netflix invested heavily in local content (producing in 40+ countries). Uber struggled in China (regulatory, cultural differences) and exited.

**Decision framework:**
- Invest in internationalization architecture (i18n, l10n, multi-currency, multi-timezone) before localization
- Hire local teams — don't assume domestic product intuitions transfer
- Regulatory research upfront — GDPR (EU), data residency laws, local payment regulations
- Start with culturally similar markets to build the muscle, then expand to dissimilar ones
- Measure per-market unit economics independently

**Common mistakes:** Assuming domestic product-market fit transfers; translating UI without adapting UX; underestimating regulatory complexity; treating all international markets as one.

---

### 9. Cold Start / Marketplace Chicken-and-Egg

**Scenario:** Building a marketplace or platform where neither side shows up without the other.

**Real-world example:** OpenTable subsidized restaurants with free hardware. Airbnb founders photographed listings themselves. Both focused supply side first.

**Decision framework:**
- Focus one side first — usually supply (because supply creates value for demand)
- Create single-player value — the product should be useful even without the network (Yelp: reviews useful without bookings)
- Start hyper-local — dominate one geography/segment before expanding (Uber: San Francisco first)
- Subsidize strategically — pay for supply until organic demand attracts more supply

**Common mistakes:** Launching in too many markets simultaneously; assuming "if we build it, they will come"; subsidizing both sides (unsustainable); measuring total users instead of liquidity.
